findings.problem: Post-intimacy consequence handling repeats the same boundary content in multiple passes (intentionality, secrecy, mutual-aid), so leverage stalls after the irreversible act instead of escalating through new pressure.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_09.md:289, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_09.md:297, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_09.md:389, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_09.md:397, and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_09.md:401, compress repeated declarations into one escalating negotiation keyed to an external trigger (dawn noise, fuel collapse, or men returning), then land the scene on a concrete action that enacts the choice.
findings.acceptance_test: PASS if the revised span at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_09.md:289-404 states each of these once (night was intentional, what to tell husbands, mutual aid rule) and then immediately moves to an action caused by that agreement; FAIL if any of the three points is restated in paraphrase without new leverage.

findings.problem: Consent exchanges overuse one-word call-and-response, creating transcript cadence and violating the style-bible cap on consecutive low-information replies.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rewrite the exchanges at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_09.md:133-139 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_09.md:227-237 so consent remains explicit but responses carry character-specific diction, interruption, or sensory context rather than repeated mono-syllabic confirms.
findings.acceptance_test: PASS if each targeted exchange contains no run longer than one one-word reply and still includes at least one explicit verbal check and one explicit verbal assent; FAIL if two or more consecutive low-information replies remain in either block.

findings.problem: Key consent/boundary lines drift into modern policy phrasing, which blunts period-authentic voice and makes Elsie and Noreen sound more interchangeable than their style-bible registers allow.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_09.md:163-164 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_09.md:397-399, keep the same consent and stop-right meaning but recast in 1962 working-class idiom with differentiated idiolect (Elsie practical/blunt; Noreen rhythmic, socially deft).
findings.acceptance_test: PASS if revised lines preserve immediate withdrawal rights while avoiding slogan-like constructions and showing clear voice separation between speakers in those two moments; FAIL if the dialogue still reads as generic policy language either character could say unchanged.

findings.problem: A recurring paragraph-start formula ("They + verb") appears far more than twice and flattens rhythmic variance in a chapter that should feel progressively less controllable.
findings.rewrite_direction: Revise starts at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_09.md:127, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_09.md:191, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_09.md:285, and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_09.md:387 to vary structural entry points (object pressure, sensory hit, or single-focalizer interior response) instead of repeated dual-subject setup.
findings.acceptance_test: PASS if the revised chapter has no sequence of more than two adjacent paragraphs beginning with "They" in the targeted section and at least three targeted paragraphs open with distinct, scene-specific anchors; FAIL if the repeated opener pattern remains dominant.

findings.problem: Elsie's stress tell (inventory counting under pressure) is repeated in near-identical form three times, reading as token-list reuse rather than evolving psychology.
findings.rewrite_direction: Keep one counting beat as signature, but at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_09.md:93 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_09.md:379 replace at least one counting instance with a different stress manifestation already canonical for Elsie (task over-specification followed by silence, or bodily check tied to fear).
findings.acceptance_test: PASS if only one of the three cited moments uses explicit counting language and the other revised moment(s) show stress through distinct behavior while preserving fuel-scarcity stakes; FAIL if all three beats still present stress primarily as counting.

findings.problem: The clipped reply "I'll." is grammatically incomplete and reads as artificial terseness, violating the constitution's anti-elliptical-clipping rule.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_09.md:221, replace the clipped fragment with a complete spoken clause that keeps urgency and character voice (for example, a full assent tied to the prior check-in).
findings.acceptance_test: PASS if the revised line at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_09.md:221 is a grammatically complete clause and preserves the consent beat's immediacy; FAIL if the line remains a clipped auxiliary fragment.

summary: Chapter 9 lands the required blackout-to-intimacy state change and avoids sanitization/fade-to-black, but it does not clear pass level because consequence beats are repeated rather than escalated, dialogue periodically slips into low-information/policy cadence, and prose relies on recurring structural formulas that flatten rhythm and voice distinctness.
